title: RollAway
description: RollAway was a map-first progressive web app built for San Francisco mobile food vendors that ranked legal, low-competition setup locations by combining foot traffic, competitor density, permit legality, and travel time into a deterministic scoring engine deployed on DigitalOcean Functions. The frontend used React 19, TypeScript, Vite, Tailwind CSS v4, and Mapbox to render a ranked pin map with spot detail sheets, while LLMs were scoped strictly to phrasing explanations grounded in precomputed signals rather than driving any business logic. The app also consolidated SF's four-agency permit process into a single guided checklist with fillable PDFs, and supported menu ingestion from text, links, images, and PDFs via a Gradient-backed extraction pipeline.

title: AI-Driven Resume Quality & ATS Analysis System
description: Built an agentic resume tailoring system that ingests a user's resume, GitHub repositories, and LinkedIn profile into a skills knowledge graph, then deploys a LangGraph multi-agent pipeline to generate ATS-optimized resumes targeted to specific job descriptions. The system employed best-of-N candidate selection with early exit optimization, LangChain integrations with Anthropic and OpenAI models, and a SQLModel ORM layer backed by SQLite locally and Supabase Postgres in production. Delivered a full-stack application with a React/TypeScript chat interface for iterative resume revision, containerized with Docker and deployed to Fly.io with Supabase JWT authentication.

title: Portfolio
description: Built a personal portfolio website to showcase data science projects, experience, and resume in a clean, fast, and fully custom interface. Architected the site as a flat collection of vanilla HTML, CSS, and ES module JavaScript files with a single source-of-truth data layer (`data/site.js`) that drives all four pages simultaneously, eliminating framework overhead and build tooling entirely. Automated GitHub repository metadata sync via Node.js scripts that fetch and merge live repo data without overwriting editorial content, and deployed the site as a static files on GitHub Pages.

title: AI Agent Policy Optimization
description: Built a reinforcement learning policy optimizer for an AI-driven HVAC contractor follow-up system, training an agent to select context-appropriate actions (check-ins, estimate nudges, objection handling, escalation, or opt-out) across multi-turn homeowner conversations. Implemented a custom simulation environment in pure Python modeling realistic homeowner states, objection types, and response probabilities, then applied policy gradient optimization to balance conversion rate against opt-out risk, customer trust, and messaging cost. The resulting policy learned to suppress low-value nudges, escalate unhappy customers to human agents, and prioritize relationship-oriented check-ins over sales pressure in cold or uncertain conversation states.

title: Diginetica: GNN vs. Flat Ranker for Session Recommendation
description: Built an end-to-end relational deep learning pipeline on the Diginetica (CIKM Cup 2016) e-commerce logs, using RelBench to convert the relational database into a heterogeneous graph — 8 node types (items, sessions, users plus view/query/click/queried-item/purchase events) and 22 edge types over 11 foreign-key relations. Engineered an `abs_time` event clock at microsecond resolution by folding intra-session millisecond offsets into the event date, enabling time-aware neighbor sampling that admits only neighbors strictly earlier than a per-row seed time so the receptive field never peeks at the future. Defined two tasks: session-conversion entity classification (binary, ~4% positive rate, AUPRC/AUROC) and next-item link prediction via intra-session leave-last-out on 218K sessions. The GNN is a two-tower heterogeneous GraphSAGE encoder (HeteroEncoder + temporal encoder) splitting into session and item towers scored by dot product, trained with sampled-softmax over in-batch and pool negatives. It is compared against an XGBoost LambdaRank ranker (`rank:ndcg`) over anchor-based 1-hop features — co-view, co-purchase, transition, category, popularity, price, is_repeat. A shared per-row candidate pool (co-view + transition + co-purchase + category + popularity backfill, built on train statistics only) lets both models rank the identical set, isolating ranking quality from candidate recall, with Optuna sweeps (persistent SQLite, median pruning) tuning both on Novel Recall@10. Evaluation decomposes Recall@10, NDCG@10, and MRR across slices — Repeat/Novel, popularity (Head/Torso/Tail), and session length — through a single masked `task.evaluate` code path, alongside structural ablations on hop depth, item-feature capacity, and scoring head (pooled dot vs. maxpool vs. ColBERT-style late interaction).

title: Idx_cont_training
description: Built an end-to-end MLOps pipeline for continuously training machine learning models on real estate listing data, integrating a live data streaming architecture with automated model retraining workflows. Implemented gradient boosting models (LightGBM, XGBoost) alongside geospatial feature engineering with GeoPandas, and custom preprocessing modules for handling missing data and outliers. The pipeline enabled real-time model updates as new listings entered the market, maintaining predictive accuracy without manual intervention.

title: Portfolio Live Editor
description: Built an Express.js proxy server that intercepted live HTTP responses from a personal portfolio site and injected a WYSIWYG editing overlay into every served page, enabling point-and-click content editing directly on the rendered site. The overlay communicated with a custom REST API to read and write changes to the source data file instantly, bypassing any build pipeline or recompilation step. This eliminated context-switching between the live site and raw files during content iteration, replacing manual edits with an in-place visual interface.

title: Real Time ML Feature Store And Quant Research Platform
description: Built a modular quantitative research platform featuring a real-time ML feature store, event-driven backtesting engine, and tax-loss harvesting rules engine, using Python, DuckDB, and Feast to process daily OHLCV data for S&P 500 equities sourced from Yahoo Finance. Engineered a six-phase pipeline spanning corporate-action-adjusted bar ingestion stored as Parquet, lot-level position tracking, wash-sale-aware harvest opportunity detection, and a mean-reversion classifier serving as a tax-loss harvesting gate. The system unified offline feature engineering with a Feast-backed feature store, enabling reproducible strategy backtests and ML-driven trade filtering across the full S&P 500 universe.

title: California Real Estate Price Prediction
description: Built a machine learning web application that predicts single-family residence prices across California using ensemble models including XGBoost and LightGBM, trained on historical real estate data with features such as location, square footage, and property characteristics. The application was deployed via a Streamlit interface with interactive visualizations powered by Plotly, allowing users to input property details and receive price predictions in real time. Model training leveraged scikit-learn pipelines for preprocessing and hyperparameter tuning, with joblib used for efficient model serialization and deployment.

title: eCommerce Session Review Analysis - Predicting Online Shopper Purchase Intent
description: Built classification models using Random Forest and XGBoost on 12,330 e-commerce browsing sessions to predict purchase intent, applying threshold tuning to maximize recall and reduce missed conversions, achieving approximately 90% accuracy. Applied K-Means clustering to segment shoppers into five behavioral archetypes, uncovering conversion rates ranging from 0.4% for bounce-prone visitors to 24.3% for high-intent consumers. Identified product browsing depth and administrative page engagement as the strongest purchase predictors using Python, scikit-learn, pandas, and seaborn.

title: Dsc207miniproject
description: Built a data science project using Python to collect, process, and visualize structured datasets through HTTP requests and pandas-based data manipulation. Applied statistical analysis and visualization techniques using matplotlib and seaborn to uncover patterns and relationships within the data. Delivered insights through a Jupyter Notebook workflow integrating the full data pipeline from ingestion to visual reporting.

title: Sleepercelmanual
description: Built a manual, notebook-based version of a sleeper cell detection pipeline using Python, pandas, and NumPy to process and evaluate network or behavioral data retrieved via the Requests library. Implemented hand-crafted feature engineering and classification logic to identify dormant or low-activity threat patterns without relying on automated ML frameworks. The project served as a ground-truth reference implementation for validating algorithmic approaches in a companion automated system.

title: SleeperCel
description: Built a full-stack AI roster building assistant for fantasy football that integrates with the public Sleeper API to surface personalized league data, roster composition, and available player recommendations. The frontend was developed using React and Vite, while the backend leveraged Python with pandas and NumPy to process player statistics and support data-driven roster decisions. The tool provides fantasy managers with a streamlined interface to evaluate roster moves by combining live Sleeper API data with analytical insights derived from structured player datasets.

title: DSC 80 Project Notebook
description: Without access to the actual notebook content, I can provide a description based on the detected libraries and context: This project applied machine learning techniques using scikit-learn to build predictive models on a structured dataset, leveraging pandas for data cleaning and feature engineering alongside matplotlib and plotly for interactive visualizations. The workflow followed a full data science pipeline including exploratory data analysis, preprocessing, model training, and evaluation. NumPy supported numerical computations throughout, while Plotly enabled dynamic visual insights into patterns and model performance.

title: UCSD Research Lab
description: Conducted research at UCSD using Jupyter Notebook and R to support ongoing investigative work under the supervision of Andre Gray. Applied statistical modeling and data analysis techniques to process and interpret research datasets, producing reproducible computational notebooks. Findings contributed to the broader research objectives of the lab, with workflows documented across both Python and R environments.

title: Bike Traffic in Cambridge Interactive Visualization
description: Built an interactive visualization of bike traffic patterns across Cambridge using JavaScript, CSS, and HTML to display real-world cyclist flow data on a dynamic map interface. Implemented filtering controls and visual encodings such as scaled markers and color gradients to communicate traffic volume and direction across different times of day. The project revealed key high-traffic corridors and peak commuting patterns throughout the Cambridge bike network.

title: Desktop Tutorial
description: This tutorial project demonstrated the foundational GitHub Desktop workflow, including initializing a repository, editing a README file, and committing changes through a graphical interface. The exercise provided hands-on practice with core version control concepts such as tracking file modifications and staging commits without using the command line. The project established a baseline understanding of Git-based project management for subsequent development work.

title: How Airbnb Tourism was impacted by the San Diego Sunshine
description: A static graph which explores Airbnb bookings and revenue throughout the year in San Diego, California. The graph shows how the number of bookings and revenue changes throughout the year, with the highest peaks in the summer months.

title: Study on the Impact of Estrus on Female Rats Throughout the Day
description: This graph is a static visualization of the body temperature across male and female mice. Used to explore differences in the daily cylce of body temperature while highlighting the impact of female estrus.

title: AllRecipes Rating Predictive Classification Model
description: This machine learning model predicts the ratings of recipes on AllRecipes.com based on features of the recipe. We created a multiclass-classifier using a RandomForest.

title: Study of the Presence of Educational Institutions and Income Throughout California
description: An exploratory data analysis of the presence of educational institutions and income throughout California. The project uses a heatmap to show the distribution of educational institutions and income throughout the state of California.

title: Image Processor
description: Built an image processor that can read in an image, apply a filter to it, and then output the processed image. The image processor can apply filters such as negate, grayscale, and edge detection. This project was built using Python.

title: Asteroid Landfall Simulation
description: Written using pandas in the Jupyter Notebook environment, this project simulates the landfall of asteroids using historical datasets, probabilistic modeling, and bootstrapping.

title: Interactive Visualization of Mice Body Temperature Across Gender
description: An interactive visualization of the body temperature across the gender of mice. The visualization allows users to explore the differences in body temperature while allowing filtering and the altering of scope. Features a tooltip which highlights Mouse ID allowing us to examine indvidual mice along with their estrus cycle( should they be female).
